
::: {.paper-table}
**Table 33. C.6 Education and self-improvement at original billing**

| Finding | Published framing / effect | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 10,000 hours → expert performance | Deliberate practice as the dominant predictor | Expertise is almost entirely a function of practice; innate differences are small | Ericsson, Krampe & Tesch-Römer 1993; popularized by Gladwell |
| Grit predicts academic and life outcomes beyond conscientiousness | r > 0.3 unique variance in early papers | A brief scale measures a trait that substantially predicts who succeeds, independent of the Big Five | Duckworth et al. [@duckworth2007] |
| Growth mindset interventions | d = 0.3–0.5 in early studies | A brief classroom intervention substantially raises achievement, especially among disadvantaged students | Dweck, Blackwell et al. |
| Brain training → fluid intelligence | d large in early reports | N-back and similar training substantially raises IQ, with far-transfer to academic and professional outcomes | Jaeggi et al. [@jaeggi2008] |
| Learning styles matching | assumed large | Tailoring instruction to a student’s “visual” vs. “auditory” style substantially improves learning | Wide belief, vanishing evidence |
| Mozart effect | d large short-term | Listening to classical music briefly substantially raises spatial reasoning; early-childhood exposure permanently raises IQ | Rauscher, Shaw & Ky [@rauscher1993] ; stronger claims in popularization |
| Nun Study — early-life writing predicts Alzheimer’s | very strong | Autobiographical complexity at age 22 substantially predicts dementia risk 60 years later | Snowdon et al. |
:::
